chunks.title: Course Information / Course Description
chunks.content:
Course Number: BICH 432 / GENE 432
Course Title: Molecular Genetics Laboratory
Section: 900
Time: Tues/Thurs 10:05am-12:55pm
Location: BICH 243
Credit Hours: 2cr

To give you a sense of what it is like to do research in an actual research laboratory, this course is set up as a comprehensive start-to-finish research project where a mutation in S. cerevisiae is introduced and its effect of growth assayed using spot plate analysis. As such, you will work on a single gene to give continuity to your laboratory experience. This course places an emphasis on effective written scientific communication. First you will learn the basics of S. cerevisiae strain construction by exploring methods in real publications, following which you will draft your own protocol to introduce a mutation into a strain of S. cerevisiae. The resulting mutant strains will be grown, and dilutions of saturated cultures will be plated on specialized media so that differences in growth compared to wild type can be assessed. You keep up-to-date records of all protocols, results, and relevant discussions obtained from each experiment and you will write a research paper-style manuscript detailing the techniques used and data generated that will be due at the end of the course to convey your results in a scholarly scientific manner.

Special Course Designation
Writing intensive (W), Cross-listed

This is a writing-based course (W), thus you must pass the writing portion of the course with a 60% in order to pass the class. An 'A' on other assignments and 'F' in the writing portion will result in a failing grade. Do not fall behind in the writing portion of this course.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: GENE 301, GENE 302, GENE 303, BIMS320/GENE320, or GENE320/BIMS320, BICH431/GENE431 or GENE431/BICH431.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Formulate your own protocols for use in the lab from published resources such as journals.
  o a. Interpretation of written work is critical for reproducibility, as well as for use in novel approaches that may be relevant to your own research. The success of any project requires the ability to read, interpret, and write about the work of others and their findings.
  o b. Generate a cohesive plan in which experiments act as puzzle pieces towards a cumulative larger goal.
• Arrange an effective written document that demonstrates your knowledge on the project and the methods therewithin. As many aspects of science require writing this class is designed to give you the experience and mental preparation for scientific writing.
  o a. Learning to author an outline for the introduction helps you organize your thoughts on topics and organize references.
  o b. Rough Draft Edits. Writing a paper in pieces helps you learn how to best approach writing a large body of work with multiple purposes.
• Demonstrate mastery of communicating important scientific information. Written communication and presentation is critical in science:
  o a. Publications - publishing of your scientific contributions, or presenting them at conferences
  o b. Grant Writing - securing funding for scientific endeavors
  o c. Leadership – communication with those you work with or work for is necessary to indicate that you can do well at your hired position
• Evaluate, edit, and offer critical feedback on scientific writing.
  o a. Science is a group collective effort, as publications are peer reviewed and grants are peer reviewed; it is critical to be able to offer professional, constructive peer evaluations with written comments.
  o b. Successful presentations often begin with written notes, outlines, speeches, etc. Successful writing is a critical part of successful oral communication.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content: There is no formal textbook for the course. Resources include but not limited to pubmed.com

chunks.title: Learning Resources
chunks.content: Available to all students is the University Writing Center for a W/C course such as ours. Please examine their website writingcenter.tamu.edu for further information as their resources are free of charge.

chunks.title: Grading Policy / GRADING, ASSIGNMENTS, & EXPECTED PARTICIPATION -
chunks.content:
IT IS YOUR RESPONSIBILITY to make sure each assignment is completed accurately and turned in on the indicated date. All assignments are due at the assigned time and late penalties will be assessed if assignments are not turned in on time. Please note, many assignment dates are indicated on the following pages in a table titled ‘Tentative Assignment Due Dates – Spring 2026' for you to reference. Remember this is tentative, should unforeseen circumstances alter our semester course schedule

MANDATORY ATTENDANCE: This course is a laboratory based course, the data collected during our wet lab sessions will be critical for writing the final paper. As such attendance to this course is MANDATORY, If more than 50% of labs are missed for the semester, whether excused or not, you will also receive a grade of zero for the course. It is not reasonable to take an in-person laboratory course and expect to pass if you are not in attendance.

COURSE PTS (Note at least 50% of course pts is considered to be 'W' – ensuring this course meets the criteria of writing intensive. These 'W' points come from the 350 pt final paper and the 400 pts attributed to the writing process such as drafts and outlines. Remember, you can't pass the course if you don't pass the written portions attributed to the ‘W' as described by the writing center. Please see the requirements if necessary - http://writingcenter.tamu.edu/)
This course will be graded on a letter scale, A 90% or above, B 80-89%, C 70-79%, D 60%-69%, F 59% or below.

Total Course Pts 1200
| Writing Section | 700: |
| :-------------- | :--- |
| Notebook Check: | 100 |
| Draft Protocol: | 50 |
| Paper Outline:  | 50 |
| Abstract:       | 50 |
| Rough Draft #1: | 100 |
| Final Draft:    | 350 |
| Exams/Quizzes/Assignments | 500: |
| Worksheets:               | 100 |
| Quizzes and Peer Review:  | 100 |
| Journal Club Presentations: | 100 |
| Midterm Exam:             | 100 |
| Final Exam:               | 100 |

Grading: Written Research Report –60% of grade
Consists of:
• Paper Outlines, Protocol Drafts, and Abstract
• Graded rough draft
• Final paper
• Two Lab Notebook Checks (First half and second half semester grading)

Rough Draft: 100 pts. In addition to peer review comments students will be given the opportunity to get feedback on their paper from the course instructor prior to turning in the final written report.
Written Report: 350 pts A written report in the form of a scientific research paper is due before the end of the semester. This report should cover the entire laboratory from start to finish in addition to previous student data concerning the 432-course student project that is data driven by undergraduates.. Presentation of findings will rely on current publications in the field, thus reading and proper citation for scientific publications is required as well. The report should include a title, an abstract, an introduction, materials and methods, results, discussion, acknowledgements, and references sections.The paper itself should be well cited with 15 or more peer reviewed citations and should consist of at least 2000 words but less than 3000 words. A document detailing the format and section requirements will be discussed in lecture and available on the class Canvas website. One rough draft will be required, the introduction and details of strain construction and spot plate assay results, the interpretation of the growth assays and presentation of findings in the context of a scientific field. A final writing assignment will require students to develop a model explaining the current state of the 432- research project. Students will illustrate a model, offer a verbal explanation of the model, and put forth future experiments and desired outcomes of those experiments. There will also be peer reviews of paper drafts occurring at two separate occasions just prior to due dates for the rough drafts. Up to four lab periods will be devoted to working on the paper- these are the Writing Workshops, and there are two separate classes devoted to journal article analysis to ensure appropriate level of student understanding. The final report will be evaluated on content, level of effort, format and organization. A printed copy will be turned in for grading, an electronic version must also be submitted via Canvas to “turn- it-in” on assignment due date to allow for verification that no plagiarism has occurred.
Lab Notebook: 100 pts. Two semester checks, 50 pts each. We will use an online lab notebook this semester, the following still applies: You will be required to complete a write-up of each laboratory exercise each lab class in your notebook. Your notebook must have a signed copy of the laboratory safety agreement (final page of syllabus, a photo upload will suffice). Along with what is written as a pre-lab, you should include all data collected, including any graphs and pictures generated, any questions specifically asked in the protocol or as a result of worksheets or homework related to the lab, and then conclusions relevant to what was learned regarding final data and the purpose stated in the pre-lab. The grade will be based not on the quality of your data but more on the completeness of your data, discussion and conclusions and on how clearly you present them. Each entry should be dated. The data should be recorded neatly, dated, titled and annotated referencing the specific steps in the associated protocol. Pictures of gels should be clearly labeled (draw arrows to bands on gel indicating which molecular species are present). The final pages of every notebook should present a working model with explanation of data obtained to put forth the model. Future experiments and pending questions pertaining to the research should also be explained. Further, because notebook skills are essential for science, to stress the importance of a good notebook, notebooks may be used for any assignments, quizzes, exams, and on the final – keep a good notebook.

Assignments - 20% of grade
Consists of:
• Worksheets
• Journal Clubs
• Peer Review x2 (will fall under 'quizzes')
Journal Club Presentations – As this course constantly strives to improve students' scientific abilities, the ability to critically read and understand scientific publications is also highlighted in this course. Publications relevant to the current 432 project are assigned as reading. Students, either individually, in groups of 2 or 4 will work together to present a specific aspect of the paper to the peers in the form of a journal club presentation. This allows students the opportunity to critically discuss a paper, with emphasis on a specific aspect – which can include a figure, table of data, type of technique used for a novel purpose, proposed model, or future implications in the field. This is challenging, to enhance student experience and allow for a learning process as part of an oral presentation. Good effort is rewarded accordingly.
Pre/Post-Lab write up: Before coming to the lab each student must write in your laboratory notebook: the title of the lab, the purpose of the lab, the reagents needed/used, and the procedure that will be followed. The procedure should be written out in enough manner that the laboratory may be performed without the use of the laboratory protocol. These will be checked at the beginning of each lab for a grade.
Peer Review Comments: The peer review process is a necessary and vital part of science research success. Thus, students will be given multiple opportunities to offer peer reviews. On two separate occasions throughout the semester, peer review groups will be assigned in which students upload the current draft of their individual paper for group members to read and comment on. In order to receive points, everyone must upload their paper on a set date/time. However, points for this assignment come from the quality of comments a student offers in response to uploaded papers from each of the group members. These worksheets are NOT simply completion grades. All comments are due on an assigned date and time as announced on Canvas.

Examinations - 20% of grade
• Quizzes
• Midterm Exams
• Final Exam
Quizzes: Quizzes are given throughout the semester to ensure that student understanding is adequately evolving throughout the semester as it pertains to the research project. Multiple quizzes will be given. Quizzes are given the first few minutes of a lab class, so being to class on time is imperative. Quizzes missed can only be made up for university excused absences as covered in the “Attendance is Mandatory" section of the syllabus.
Midterm: A midterm exam will serve as an example of what type of thought and understanding are necessary for the final. These questions will be in the form of short answers, calculations, multiple-choice, and matching based on topics related to the research project, reading assignments, and to lab techniques used throughout the semester. The midterm is a timed exam, open laboratory notebook, and calculator.
Final Exam: One of the last days of regularly scheduled lab class (see schedule), there will be a final examination. The exam will be an open laboratory notebook exam and will include a series of questions (short answers, calculations, multiple-choice, matching) from the topics and techniques covered in the lab as well as from the protocols, reading assignments, and lecture material.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late Penalty: You will have 20% of the assignment grade deducted per day for late assignments. Some assignments are due at the start of class, if not turned in at that time, they will be considered late. No assignments will be accepted after 5 days unless appropriate university-excused absences are documented. For all university-excused absences, appropriate paperwork must be presented for verification.

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: BICH/GENE 432 LABORATORY SAFETY REGULATIONS
chunks.content:
The following safety regulations must be always obeyed to ensure your safety and the safety of others. Students not complying with these rules may face grade penalties and/or dismissal from the laboratory. If you have any questions, consult your instructor.

A. GENERAL GUIDELINES
1. Where you sit will be your assigned seat for the semester.
2. Everyone is responsible for the experiments. Come prepared by reading protocols and required reading in advance!! Activities will be started immediately, while explanations and discussion sessions will occur as time permits. COME PREPARED TO ASK QUESTIONS.
3. Equipment in this and neighboring labs is shared so please treat it well. If you do not know how to use a piece of equipment, ask a staff person. Obey lab rules, such as ensuring all drawer contents are accounted for and stored properly, no extra materials are left lying around. Leave all equipment in good working order. If there are problems, tell us so we can fix them!
4. Leave the lab better than you found it. Wash your own glassware, clean up your work area, inform instructor/TA of reagents or materials that are running out, etc.
5. KEEP UP WITH NOTEBOOK ENTRIES EVERY DAY...otherwise, data will be lost!
6. All reagents and samples should be saved and must be labeled with the date, your initials and Pair #, and WHAT IT IS. Items not labeled sufficiently may be discarded.
7. Store things in appropriate places! For DNA, protein samples, reaction mixes and certain buffers, store either in a designated refrigerator or in a designated freezer at – 200C in the storage box provided to your group unless otherwise noted. Note storage places in your notebook.

B. LABORATORY SAFETY
Biochemical/Molecular Biological research involves the use of many different chemical reagents. You should always exercise caution when dealing with any chemical. However, some chemicals require more careful handling than others. Extra precautions for using such chemicals will be noted in your lab manual. We will provide safety glasses and gloves for your convenience.
Each student must read, sign and turn in a safety agreement before working in this laboratory. A copy of this agreement is attached to this syllabus for your reference. You should pay close attention to the safety requirements for each lab period. These are detailed in your lab manual. It is your responsibility to understand and obey all the safety requirements. If you have specific safety questions or concerns, please ask your instructor or TA. Specific safety precautions you need to follow:
• No eating, drinking, or chewing gum in the laboratory.
You must wear closed toe shoes in the laboratory
No handling contact lenses.
• No smoking in the laboratory or in the Bio/Bio Bldg.
Know the location of fire extinguishers, eyewash stations, safety showers and fire alarms.
• Long hair must be pulled back
Never put glass in the regular trash, use special "glass only" containers.
If an accident occurs, immediately notify your instructor or TA.
The Emergency Number on Campus is 9-911
The Campus Health Center number is 5-1511
An important campus safety resource is the Environmental Health and Safety Department web page located at http://ehsd-online.tamu.edu/. Among other things, you can access Material Safety Data Sheets from this site that describe the precautions you should take when working with some of the chemicals we will be using in this lab. Safety information and disposal of hazardous chemicals used in lab will be indicated in the protocols and therefore should be written in your lab notebook.

C. GOOD LAB TECHNIQUES
1. Work on ice unless otherwise directed. It slows degradation of macromolecules.
2. Many reagents settle on storage, so mix them! All frozen solutions need to be thawed and mixed before using.
3. ENZYMES DO OUR WORK. They are stable as glycerol solutions at -20oC. Keep them in the freezer as much as possible. Only remove them in the appropriate blue - 200C freezer blocks. DO NOT ALLOW ENZYME STOCKS TO WARM! They are EXPENSIVE!
4. When pipetting small amounts, check loaded pipet tip before and after the evacuation to assure that solution has gotten into the reaction mix. After addition, mix reaction solution gently but thoroughly: you can pipet total volume up and down OR vortex gently and flash spin to return reaction to the bottom of the tube.

The next two pages of this syllabus include duplicate copies of the lab safety agreement. You need to read through the rules and ensure you understand safety expectations. You will sign both copies, one will be turned into the instructor for departmental files, and one will be taped to the front inside cover of your lab notebook. I can make copies for all the students on the first day of class.

The following safety regulations must be always obeyed to ensure your safety and the safety of others. Students not complying with these rules may face grade penalties and/or dismissal from the laboratory. If you have any questions, consult your instructor.

1. Never work in the laboratory unless a teaching assistant (TA) or laboratory supervisor is present. Follow all safety guidelines outlined in the laboratory manual or otherwise communicated to you by your instructor. Never perform unauthorized experiments.
2. Eating, drinking, and smoking are never allowed in the laboratory. Never taste chemicals.
3. Carefully read all instructions and thoroughly plan your work prior to beginning.
4. Learn emergency procedures and know the locations and operation of the nearest eye wash, shower, and chemical cleanup materials.
5. Feet must be protected by closed-toe shoes at all times in the laboratory. Bare feet, sandals, and open-toed shoes are not permissible. Confine long hair when instructed.
6. Aisles must be free of clutter. Thus, backpacks and other personal articles should be secured beneath your workstation.
7. Dispose of waste chemicals only as instructed by your laboratory instructor or TA.
8. Needles, glass or other sharp objects must be disposed of in special containers as instructed by your TA. They must not be disposed of in the regular trash receptacles.
9. Safety glasses must be worn when indicated in the lab manual or when instructed.
10. Notify your TA or lab supervisor immediately of any injury, accident or spill in the lab.

I realize that all chemicals are potentially dangerous; therefore, I will exercise care in handling them. If I am unsure of the potential hazards of any chemical, I will discuss this with my instructor prior to use. If I have a medical condition such as, but not limited to, hypo- or hyperglycemia, diabetes, epilepsy, pregnancy, heart ailments, or any other medical condition which may cause sudden loss of consciousness, I certify that I am under a doctor's care and that my doctor has given me explicit permission to participate in this laboratory course. I will inform my instructor of my condition at the beginning of the semester, or as soon as I am aware of the existence of the medical condition.

I have read and understand the BICH/GENE 432 lab safety regulations and will comply with these regulations for my own safety and the safety of others. I understand that failure to comply with these regulations may result in grade penalties or expulsion from the course.

Sign both copies of this form. Retain one copy in your laboratory notebook. Your laboratory supervisor will collect and retain the other signed copy.

Name (print) _______________________ Fall/Spring/Summer 20___ UIN_____________________

Signature ________________________________ Date _______________

Lab Instructor Name (print) ____________________________________

chunks.title: Course Summary
chunks.content:
BICH 432 / GENE 432 | Molecular Genetics Laboratory | Spring 2026
Instructor: Dr. Melissa Koff | mkoff@tamu.edu
Meets: Tues/Thurs 10:05am-12:55pm | BICH 243
Topics: H3K4 methylation, Epistasis, Lithium Acetate Transformation of Yeast, linearized plasmids, set1 mutants, Single Colony Purification, Patch Plating, Replica Plating, Colony PCR, PCR troubleshooting, Spot Plate Assay, SET1 and set1Δ strains, mutation in S. cerevisiae, S. cerevisiae strain construction, macromolecules, enzymes, protocol drafting, figure making, nomenclature, abstract writing, discussion sections, lab notebook, pipette calibration, chemical reagents, lab safety, emergency procedures, waste disposal, good lab techniques, pipetting, data analysis, experimental design, scientific writing, oral presentation, critical thinking, problem-solving, peer review, grant writing, scientific publications, scientific communication
Tools: pubmed.com, EndNote, Canvas, calculator, online lab notebook
Prerequisites: GENE 301, GENE 302, GENE 303, BIMS320/GENE320, or GENE320/BIMS320, BICH431/GENE431 or GENE431/BICH431
